prompt:
The media NAS hit 94% again and I already know why: the same renders and raw exports copied into five project folders by five different people. I've been limping along with a bash one-liner (find piped into md5sum piped into sort) and it's embarrassing. It hashes every byte of four terabytes even though a file with a unique size cannot possibly have a twin. It once sat blocked on a stale FIFO in somebody's scratch dir for an entire weekend. It reports hardlinks as duplicates — they're the same inode, deleting one frees nothing. And the day someone dropped a symlink pointing back up the tree it walked forever. I want a real library we can hang a CLI and a TUI off. Module go-dupfinder, package dupfinder, this repo, stdlib only.

Entry point: Find(root string, opts Options) (*Report, error).

Options{Workers int; MinSize int64; Progress func(stage Stage, path string)}. Workers is mandatory, >= 1, error otherwise — it caps how many files are being hashed at once. And it's not just a cap: with at least Workers files waiting to hash, all Workers must actually be busy simultaneously — I've been burned by a "pool" that drained one size class at a time. MinSize skips regular files smaller than that (a file of exactly MinSize stays in); zero-byte files are ALWAYS skipped regardless — a thousand empty .lock files are not savings. Progress is optional: when set, workers call it with StageHashStart as they begin reading a file and StageHashEnd when they finish it (Stage is a string type; constants StageHashStart = "hash-start", StageHashEnd = "hash-end"); it may be called from many goroutines at once, that's the caller's problem.

Walking: root must be an existing directory, error otherwise. Recurse from there; only regular files participate. Symlinks are never followed and never reported — file or directory, they don't exist as far as the scan is concerned, and a symlink loop must not hang us. FIFOs, sockets, devices: skipped without ever being opened. Hardlinks: same device+inode is ONE file — collapse aliases onto the lexicographically smallest relative path; a duplicate group needs at least two DISTINCT files.

The whole point — size first: only open and hash files whose size collides with another distinct file's. A unique-size file is never opened; chunks of the archive aren't even readable by the scan account and that must not matter. If a file we genuinely need to hash can't be read, fail the scan with an error that names it. Content hash is sha256, lowercase hex.

Output: Report{Groups []Group; WastedBytes int64}, Group{Size int64; Hash string; Paths []string}. Paths are relative to root with forward slashes, sorted within each group. Groups are sorted by Size descending — biggest wins first — with ties broken by first path. WastedBytes is the sum over groups of Size*(len(Paths)-1). Same tree in, same report out, regardless of Workers — byte for byte.

dupfinder_test.go and concurrency_test.go are the acceptance contract; do not touch them. go test -race -timeout 30s ./... has to pass. And structure it like you mean it — the walk, the hashing pool, and report assembly do not belong in one file.
